comments.body:
Closed-children evidence (criterion 1): all 12 work-children are status=closed, each landed via a Gerrit change with Verified+1 AND LLM-Review+1:
  - a41f-3153-9292-4e76 = Gerrit 564: closed
  - b55f-639d-b4bd-4222 = Gerrit 565: closed
  - 319a-65a5-9b1d-45b2 = Gerrit 566: closed
  - eba7-4315-0f06-42d4 = Gerrit 567: closed
  - e205-cf65-aea8-42e9 = Gerrit 568(+577 fix): closed
  - 9d81-2afd-5b67-4bf2 = Gerrit 569: closed
  - 6755-689b-72c2-4dac = Gerrit 570: closed
  - f088-ef68-9518-4833 = Gerrit 571: closed
  - 0435-8dce-4e9f-4121 = Gerrit 572(+578 fix): closed
  - e866-0bdb-94e3-41e6 = Gerrit 573: closed
  - 235a-e6c1-e034-4a75 = Gerrit 574: closed
  - b5a4-e065-a252-4bc5 = Gerrit 575: closed
All 12 closed: True. The 13th child (a454, the parked 'idea') was detached to standalone (relates_to kept) per the epic AC 'lower-value work captured as an idea, not shipped'. All 12 changes (564-575) + 2 follow-ups (577,578) MERGED to main.

compiled_state.description:
## Context

A documentation audit (final unified report, evaluated at commit `2272d68ca` =
`origin/main`) surfaced front-door defects and factual drift in rebar's docs. Every
finding was re-validated against `origin/main` before filing this epic; one audit
finding (that `docs/config.md` mis-describes the config-file format) was **REFUTED**
— the loader matches the doc exactly — and is deliberately excluded.

Our audiences are human clients, LLM clients, human contributors, and LLM
contributors. The two highest-leverage moves are (a) fixing the immediate defects
that break onboarding/trust, and (b) adding cheap **drift-proofing** so the same
class of staleness cannot silently return — the true lever on maintenance burden.

### Validated findings this epic remediates
- P0: README `session-log` syntax is invalid (missing subcommand); README quickstart
  is not actually one ticket end-to-end (hard-coded `reel-lot-tea`, separate Python
  ticket, MCP shows config only).
- P1: dependency count + `mcp` floor wrong in README and `docs/config.md` (truth: 3
  base deps, `mcp>=1.9`); `docs/output-schemas.md` points at a moved source path and
  describes a removed bash shell-out; stale bash-engine language in CI comments +
  `leaf.py`.
- P2: `docs/README.md` index is incomplete (4 unlinked living docs) and mixes living
  references with historical evidence.

### Reuse note (avoids duplicate work)
- `scripts/probe-rebar.sh` already implements the full install→…→close golden path;
  it just is not wired into CI.
- `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` + its CI drift gate is a proven **generator +
  drift-gate** pattern to template for new generated references.

## Success Criteria
- Every validated defect above is corrected against `origin/main`.
- New CI gates make quickstart-command rot and docs-index drift fail the build
  automatically (less manual doc upkeep).
- The docs index is complete and clearly separates living reference from historical
  evidence, with an intent-based router for the four audiences.
- Volatile command/tool surfaces move toward generated references so they cannot go
  stale by hand.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All child tickets are closed (each with green CI / `Verified +1`).
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass on the assembled stack. This
      aggregate integration check is owned at the EPIC level (not any single child):
      the executor runs it on the full assembled stack before landing, as the
      parent-level close gate.
- [ ] The refuted config-format finding is NOT acted on (no spurious change).
- [ ] Lower-value inventory work (Python-API / config-key) is captured as an `idea`,
      not shipped speculatively.
- [ ] The golden-path CI gate runs the **literal commands extracted from the README quickstart fenced block** (not merely an independent probe with its own syntax), so a wrong command *printed* in the README — e.g. the `session-log` missing its `append` verb — fails the build. This is the mechanism that makes command *text* rot (not just executability) a hard CI failure, and doubles as the end-to-end new-user onboarding validation (CI follows the README exactly as a cold reader would).
- [ ] Docs-index completeness/drift fails the build (docs-index CI gate `f088`): every
      living/normative `docs/*.md` is linked from the index, and the relative links
      the remediation touches (index entries + inbound links to docs relocated under
      `docs/archive/` by `9d81`) resolve. Scope is bounded to index completeness and
      the links this epic manages — it is the enforcement arm of the P2 finding, not a
      general site-wide linter.
- [ ] Generated CLI + MCP references drift-gate against their live registries.

### Drift-proofing mechanism — how command *text* rot fails the build (closes G6/F4)
The audit gap is that testing command *executability* (running `probe-rebar.sh`, which uses its own internally-correct syntax) does NOT catch a wrong command *printed in the README*. So the golden-path gate (ticket `contained-dendritic-angora`/0435) is scoped to **extract the fenced command block(s) from the README quickstart and execute those exact lines** in a throwaway repo. A stale/invalid README command then exits non-zero and fails CI. This is both the text-accuracy drift guard (G6) and the concrete end-to-end onboarding validation (F4): a new user copy-pasting the README is exactly what CI now does.

### Shared-file edit ordering (closes P5)
Several children edit `README.md` (session-log fix `a41f`, quickstart repair `b55f`, dep-count `319a`, catalog-trim `b5a4`). These are **serialized through the review stack in dependency order** — each change is built on the prior one (a41f → b55f → 319a → … → b5a4), so there is no concurrent same-file edit; `b5a4` additionally `depends_on` the generated-reference tickets (`e866`, `235a`) it links to. The stack order IS the observable ordering proxy: a later change rebases on the earlier landed change, never races it.

The SAME single-linear-stack serialization covers the other shared files: `.github/workflows/test.yml` (edited by `0435`, `e205`, `e866`, `235a`, `f088`) and `docs/README.md` (edited by `9d81`, `6755`) are each modified by exactly one change at a time in stack order — every change is built on the prior landed one, so there is never a concurrent same-file edit to reconcile. Execution is a single agent driving one relation-chain, so the dependency order and the stack order are the same total order.

file_impact.reason: front-door defects: session-log syntax, quickstart, dep count

compiled_state.title: Documentation remediation: fix front-door defects, drift-proof the docs, clarify taxonomy
